modules.description: The 5000 level course is designed to provide students with a leveling refresher of basic math and physics concepts common to topics such as communications, sensors, electromagnetic warfare, etc. This course also supplies the tools necessary to succeed in the higher-level courses.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will summarize how key mission systems physics fit into a modern real-world battlespace to provide a mission-focused context for how to test those systems.
TLO 2. The student will interpret vocabulary associated with describing categories of mission systems.
TLO 3. The student will cite the test fundamentals related to a variety of mission systems, including more modern frameworks, used for aircraft and spacecraft systems.
TLO 4. The student will contrast the difference between types of testing.
TLO 5. The student will implement an existing test methodology (test card) to a specific mission system.
TLO 6. The student will compare the results to predicted behavior stated in the system documentation.
TLO 7. The student will deduce what could have accounted for the anomalous behavior recorded during the given test.
TLO 8. The student will categorize deficiencies to communicate mission relevance.
TLO 9. The student will prioritize test results to meet the warfighter need.

events.description: This 4-hour academic block is an overview of the essential math, physics, and engineering knowledge that span many mission systems.

The lab introduces the student to test methodologies for evaluating integrated avionics systems in the F-16 UTD. The student is exposed to an F-16 Operational Flight Program (OFP) with a deficiency, enabled to learn about the OFP update process, and required to evaluate the OFP to perform limited regression testing to determine if new deficiencies were inadvertently created.

tlos: TLO 1. Understand and use the language of data and machine learning scientists, and how this applies to data-driven mission systems in the DoD.
TLO 2. Understand aspects of velocity, volume, value, variety and veracity of big data-generating sources.
TLO 3. Understand the latest tools and technologies for storing, parsing and analyzing big data.
TLO 4. Understand KPIs of data-driven mission systems and know when to select the corresponding KPI to the desired outcome.
TLO 5. Apply protocols of T&E to verify data-driven mission systems performance.
TLO 6. Apply and implement techniques for large scale data analysis in Python.
TLO 7. Apply adaptive control techniques and model-free algorithms to control mission systems.
TLO 8. Collect and analyze data from mission systems using modern techniques and software.
TLO 9. Identify and analyze risks in DDMS, such as vulnerabilities in communication networks, errors in control algorithms, or sensor miscalibration.

events.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Decision Making for Mission Systems Lecture
events.description: This block will cover data-driven testing for mission systems, including a broad scope of topics that include data science, uncertainty quantification, test & evaluation, statistical hypothesis testing, and decision making. Topics covered in the 1-week block include exploratory data analysis, data visualization, structured vs unstructured data, streaming vs. post-mission data, meta-data tagging, and structured inputs and data pipelines. It will also cover key performance indicators (KPIs), system performance and predictive modelling, validation and verification in data-driven test, statistical comparisons, Experimental design, adversarial machine learning & resilience strategies, and software for T&E of data-driven mission systems. Additionally, the block will cover probabilistic reasoning, reinforcement learning, autonomous control with RL (model-free and model-based), ethical considerations, data and decision fusion techniques, and considerations for tactical vs. operational vs. strategic environments.
This simulator will be a capstone event for data-driven testing of mission systems. It involves a hands-on exercise applying the skills learned throughout the program related to data-driven test.

prerequisites.name: Data-Driven Analysis and Control for Mission Systems Lecture
prerequisites.notes: Name in prerequisite listing differs from event header; PDF lists as 'Data-Driven Analysis and Control for Mission Systems Lecture'

modules.description: The 6000-level introduces the students to common sensors and navigation suites found in modern aircraft and spacecraft. The physics, functions and parameters are explained along with the processes for testing components before introducing them into a platform, and ultimately into a kill web.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will summarize how key mission systems physics fit into a modern real-world battlespace to provide a mission-focused context for how to test those systems.
TLO 2. The student will interpret vocabulary associated with describing categories of mission systems.
TLO 3. The student will cite the test fundamentals related to a variety of mission systems, including more modern frameworks, used for aircraft and spacecraft systems.
TLO 4. The student will contrast the difference between types of testing.
TLO 5. The student will implement an existing test methodology (test card) to a specific mission system.
TLO 6. The student will compare the results to predicted behavior stated in the system documentation.
TLO 7. The student will deduce what could have accounted for the anomalous behavior recorded during the given test.
TLO 8. The student will categorize deficiencies to communicate mission relevance.
TLO 9. The student will prioritize test results to meet the warfighter need.

events.description: This 9-hour academic lecture course provides students with the theoretical background necessary to understand how ultraviolet, visible, and infrared electromagnetic energy is used in military applications. Several fundamental types of EO systems are presented, and their specific applications are discussed. Measures of performance and figures of merit for various systems are presented as well as specific ground and flight test methodologies used to evaluate performance.

This 8-hour academic lecture course builds upon the Mission Systems Fundamentals lecture and provides the academic knowledge necessary to apply those fundamentals to development and test of military RF sensors. The student is introduced to radio frequency limitations, signal processing, RADAR technical measures, and mission-oriented designs specifically associated with military RADARs such as pulsed, pulse-Doppler, remote sensing and moving target detection.

These blocks build on the RADAR academic knowledge with a hands-on laboratory and one hands-on simulator (6121S or 6122S). The first RADAR lab uses the TPS LabVolt laboratory RADAR to demonstrate theoretical RADAR/test concepts as well as basic RADAR displays (A-Scope, and PPI).
The RADAR simulator occurs in the F-22 Software Integration Lab (SIL) for cleared students. Students learn basic and advanced RADAR test techniques for A/A and A/G RADAR.
The RADAR simulator occurs in the F-16 UTD for uncleared students. Students learn basic and advanced RADAR test techniques for A/A and A/G RADAR.

prerequisites.name: RADAR Simulator for Cleared Students (US Only)

events.description: This mission is designed to introduce the student to the differences between visible (TV), infrared (IR), and ground mapping RADAR sensors in the flight environment and to introduce several flight test techniques to evaluate the sensors both qualitatively and quantitatively. Knowledge gained during the EO ground test will be applied to operate a Targeting Pod in an inflight environment. The Sensors Flight provides data to support a written report due 5 business days after the last team sortie.

prerequisites.name: RADAR Simulator for Cleared Students (US Only)

events.description: The Sensors Flight provides data to support a written report due 5 business days after the last team sortie.

prerequisites.name: RADAR Simulator for Cleared Students (US Only)

tlos: TLO 1. The student will recall the critical tenants of space communication.

events.description: This lecture covers RF and Communications systems fundamentals to prepare the students for the Communications and RF exercise. This lecture series will discuss the basics of data encoding, the link equation, antenna fundamentals, modulation techniques and multiple access techniques.

The hands-on lab demonstrating RF and Communications systems fundamentals will be executed in combination with the Space Communications lecture. It will prepare the students for the Communications and RF exercise. The students will also gain familiarization with the software defined radio program, Hack RF equipment, and antenna connectivity. The students will gain familiarization with communications and antenna measures and measurement techniques. The demonstrations will step the students through a build-up of activities including RF Fundamentals using Software Defined Radios, Antennas and Common RF sources; Polarization; Signal interference and EW Measures; Antenna Patterns: Mainlobes / Sidelobes.

The exercise is the integration event for Communications and RF instructions. The students will be presented a communications and RF scenario. They will work in small teams to develop the relevant sections of a detailed test plan which they will submit as a deliverable. They will then execute their approved test plan, collect relevant communications, and RF data, analyze their results, and submit a limited evaluation as a final graded assessment. The scope is estimated to be one afternoon of scenario presentation, RF lab equipment issue, Q&A, and deliverables overview. Two afternoons are scheduled for test plan writing and one afternoon for feedback on Test Plan and corrections. Two afternoons are slated for test execution, data collection and analysis. Two afternoons are scheduled for writing of the final assessment against the mission requirements.

tlos: TLO 1. Understand navigation theory and underlying principles.
TLO 2. Analyze reference frames in relation to air and space navigation systems.
TLO 3. Describe Inertial Navigation Systems, Inertial Measurement Systems, and methods to verify their performance.

events.description: This 8-hour academic lecture Phase introduces aircraft navigation systems, including basic theory of operation, positioning information characteristics and basic test methodology. Topics include the basic principles of navigating across earth's surface, geodetic reference frames, ground-based radio navigation, Coriolis acceleration, inertial navigation, space-based satellite navigation with special emphasis on Global Positioning System applications, and integrated navigation and guidance systems using Kalman filtering techniques. The emphasis here is on understanding the fundamental principles of operation, what information is provided to the integrated navigation solution of the platform, and the limitations and potentials of these systems as they apply to systems testing and evaluation. There is also considerable discussion of the errors and analysis of large sets of navigation data.

events.eventName: Navigation and State Estimation Oral Report
events.description: Students will be assigned groups to analyze example navigation performance data provided by the instructor in the form of a notional navigation flight test program with specific requirements and performance specifications to be assessed. The students will present their results, analysis, conclusions, and recommendations to an audience composed of simulated stakeholders including the systems program office, contractor engineers, and operational testers.

modules.moduleName: Component Systems Exam and Special Topics
tlos: TLO 1. The student will synthesize mission systems information.

events.description: This test covers all the applicable component systems taught throughout SY 6000.

prerequisites.name: Navigation and State Estimation Oral Report
Navigation and State Estimation Oral Report

events.description: This is a number of presentations on cutting-edge test techniques and technical details currently in execution throughout the Air Force Test Center.

modules.description: The 7000-level series provides platform mission systems knowledge. Students will learn how to use software development and cyber security as foundations for executing and reporting on avionics tests. Students also receive introductions to the data networks that link components into a system and to the visual interfaces which present information to operators. Instruction is also provided on how weapons integrate with systems on a platform. Theory, mission application, test methodologies, and case studies are included in each topic and provide the foundational education for evaluating integrated component systems at the platform level culminating in the systems practical examination.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will compare the results to predicted behavior stated in the system documentation.
TLO 2. The student will deduce what could have accounted for the anomalous behavior recorded during the given test.
TLO 3. The student will prioritize subject matter expert feedback to reinforce curriculum knowledge.
TLO 4. The student will examine the electromagnetic spectrum and research how it is manipulated/interpreted to communicate/relay information.
TLO 5. The student will correlate the fundamental properties of physics exploited by individual mission systems and infer how those properties either enable or limit information for the warfighter.
TLO 6. The student will categorize deficiencies to communicate mission relevance.
TLO 7. The student will integrate key mission systems into a modern battlespace to provide a mission-focused context for how to test those systems.
TLO 8. The student will coordinate with testers to perform an evaluation using the electromagnetic spectrum and formulate how it is manipulated/interpreted to communicate/relay information.
TLO 9. The student will combine the fundamental properties of physics exploited by individual mission systems and integrate those properties to generate information for the warfighter.
TLO 10. The student will justify risk assessment.
TLO 11. The student will determine overall system ability and specification compliance.

events.description: This 2-hour academic lecture phase is a survey in the process of developing human machine interfaces. Benefits and challenges of each display type are examined and topics including resolution, touchscreen displays, helmet alignment, cockpit mapping, and test methodologies are presented.

This is a 3-hour academic overview of the types of documentation and specifications common in software and networks mission systems testing. The MIL-STD-1760 interface and MIL-STD-1553 data bus protocol is introduced as well as other military and commercial data busses. This block introduces the various software development and test methodologies used by defense industry partners and the DoD for aircraft, space and cyber systems, and how requirements definition, development, and test approaches differ between legacy and more modern development frameworks. It focuses on how to create a software test plan that meets specification, performance, maintenance, and quality requirements while effectively managing the tradeoffs between risk, quality, and cost. Finally, the block introduces the DevSecOps cycle, Open Mission Systems fundamentals, and continuous integration and continuous development concepts as applied to software testing of aircraft and spacecraft systems. This block also discusses vehicles as a system of systems, then decomposes individual systems using the design process down to specific mission systems, mission essential functionality, and cyber-physical systems technologies. This provides a context necessary to understand how information flow within cyber-physical systems and physical technology shapes integrated avionics/regression testing as well as cyber vulnerability analysis.

These labs reinforce academic knowledge acquired in the lecture with a 1-hour lab conducted with 4 students at a time. Students can manipulate code on a stand-alone computer to illustrate vulnerabilities such as buffer overflows and race conditions. They are also exposed to automated testing using CI/CD pipelines. Additionally, students demonstrate the performance differences between programming languages (compiled and interpreted), virtualization technologies (virtual machines and containers), Internet Protocol (IP) network data transfers (TCP and UDP), and cryptographic algorithms (AES and RSA) to better internalize the tradeoffs and promise of the cutting-edge computer science currently being used in modern mission systems architectures.

This 2-hour lecture is an introduction to the unique testing considerations for unmanned platforms from a mission systems point of view. Subjects covered will include RPA common classifications, system components and traits, and unique test considerations.

TLO 10. The student will justify risk assessment.
TLO 11. The student will determine overall system suitability and specification compliance.

events.description: This 8-hour academic lecture course introduces students to the fundamental aspects of air-to-ground and air-to-air weapons and weapons integration testing. Topics include weapon components, guided weapons delivery testing, guidance modes, release point and impact point calculation, error budgets, safe separation, DT/OT teaming, some specialized weapons testing and instrumentation.
The 2-hour field trip to the Edwards AFB weapons load hangar gives students hands-on experience with many of the weapons they may encounter during a test campaign.
A 1-hour MIB outlines the flight and mission control room events.

For the simulator event, students will be issued flight test weapon cards to practice systems test conduct, as well as discuss system management, error propagation, and systems test safety. Student teams will practice test/control division of duties and responsibilities, while offering a first look at the F-16 weapons delivery event. CSO/P/RPA students will complete the simulator session prior to flight with ABM/FTEs.

The sortie will consist of three main areas: 1) Operational release of inert GBU-12 with self-lasing to a stationary target. 2) JDAM regression testing and DLZ analysis. 3) F-16 avionics regression testing. During the sortie, students will sharpen their applied weapons test conduct foundations and will be introduced to weapons telemetry in the control room.
Students will be introduced to weapons telemetry in the control room.

events.eventName: F-16 Weapons Delivery Domain Integration MIB
F-16 Weapons Delivery Domain Integration Working Group
events.description: FTC students will work closely with STC students to plan and execute a precision Time-On-Target GBU-12 weapon delivery to correspond with commercial satellite EO/IR pass. FTC and STC students will be scheduled for a 1-hour mission planning cell at minimum.

This sortie is designed to demonstrate close integration with the Space Test Course Systems Practical and give select students a broadening experience for working with agencies beyond their background domain. Pilots selected should be primarily those with an F-16 background (primary) or those with a background dropping GBU-12s (secondary). FTC students will also accomplish JDAM regression and DLZ analysis, along with any other regression cards in the time and fuel remaining. FTC students will assist STC students with any post-flight analysis.

events.eventName: F-16 Weapons Domain Integration Control Room
events.description: FTEs or CSOs selected should be those with previous control room experience or weapons delivery experience.
Students will give an oral debrief of their JDAM DLZ error analysis as a group using relevant data collected during the F-16 flight (and sim if applicable).

events.description: There is a 1-hour MIB prior to the flight.
A 1.5-hour F-15E ground school prior to the flight.

The purpose of the F-15E Systems Evaluation is to expose the student to a highly missionized manned aircraft cockpit. Students will have an opportunity to evaluate an operational terrain following RADAR system, advanced AESA RADAR integration, unique flight test avionics, and multi-seat fighter human systems integration. The F-15E systems evaluation sortie will be tailored to the students' experience/background, with debrief focusing on DT/OT relationships, systems workload and mission utility evaluations, as well as Eglin/40 FLTS unique deficiency reporting tools and methods.

The purpose of the MQ-9 Evaluation Flight is for US students to demonstrate execution and debriefing of advanced sensor test techniques. Students will observe imagery obtained against non-traditional targets used for engineering measurements and collect qualitative usability data for a new sensor interface. Students are expected to analyze, evaluate, and debrief the data gathered during the execution event.

events.description: Students receive an MQ-9 controls and displays orientation in a ground control segment (GCS) simulator.

F-16 Weapons Domain Integration Flight (select P)
F-16 Weapons Domain Integration Control Room (select CSO/FTE)

events.description: This is an evaluation event for the ABM/CSO/RPA and select FTE/P students' Mission Systems curricula. Students are required to select appropriate targets for sensor and weapons testing, develop a mission profile and cards to accomplish a limited developmental test and evaluation of the MQ-9 sensors and ground weapons delivery system. The mission phase is conducted using either a real MQ-9 or GCS simulator depending on aircraft availability.

F-16 Weapons Domain Integration Flight (select P)
F-16 Weapons Domain Integration Control Room (select CSO/FTE)

events.description: This is the evaluation event for the test pilot and FTE Mission Systems curricula. Students are provided a test plan to evaluate a 'new' F-16 RADAR OFP. This OFP also requires a limited safety of flight regression. Pilots and FTEs will work as a team to research system operation, write test cards, execute the mission (FTE directing from control room), and provide an expanded oral debrief of results after the mission.

events.description: This practical will integrate the material presented in the mission systems platform course. Students will execute the exercise and complete a written report and oral presentation. Students will develop the test approach based on a scenario provided. Instructors will provide limited coaching and students will be expected to work in small teams to complete event. Multi-domain aspects of T&E will be considered. The project will integrate and reinforce the materials presented up to this point in the curricula and give the students an opportunity to execute a test project from beginning to end. Students will be tasked to evaluate a relevant space system. Students will be required to develop a test plan for review and approval. Students will then conduct the test using the assets available for the evaluation, analyze the data and draft a final report of the results. The scope of the exercise will be limited in order allow execution within the time constraints of the block.

modules.description: The 8000-level series is designed to integrate physics and system specific knowledge from the 6000 and 7000 series. Academics focus on mission systems above the platform level, such as Electromagnetic Warfare, Sensor Fusion, Autonomy and Datalinks. The 8000-level disciplines include an Integrated Systems Lab, and an EW simulator.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will integrate key mission systems into a modern battlespace to provide a mission-focused context for how to test those systems.
TLO 2. The student will coordinate with testers to perform an evaluation using the electromagnetic spectrum and formulate how it is manipulated/interpreted to communicate/relay information.
TLO 3. The student will combine the fundamental properties of physics exploited by individual mission systems and integrate those properties to generate information for the warfighter.
TLO 4. The student will justify risk assessment.
TLO 5. The student will determine overall system ability and specification compliance.

events.description: This 3-hour academic lecture introduces students to the fundamental concepts of communications and datalinks systems in modern aircraft. The course reviews the types of information that is communicated using communications technologies, introduces the basic technical performance measures shared by digital communications systems, fundamental test techniques used for all communications systems, and introduces datalink test fundamentals.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will be able to describe the functions served by a fusion system to an 'integrated system'.
TLO 2. The student will be able to describe some advantages and disadvantages of a fusion system.
TLO 3. The student will be able to define a simple fusion model and the concepts of data alignment, correlation, estimation, and identification.
TLO 4. The student will be able to describe the role of sensor management in a fusion system.
TLO 5. The student will be able to describe approaches to testing sensor fusion.
TLO 6. The student will be able to describe some common measures of fusion engine performance.

events.description: This 2-hour academic lecture introduces data fusion models, methods, examples, and test techniques. The course builds on individual systems and sensors knowledge to teach how sensors can be integrated and fused into a user-friendly interface.
A 2-hour laboratory is held in the F-35 simulator for US students. The Eglin F-35 simulator gives the students hands-on experience using modern integrated avionics.

prerequisites.name: US Only Briefings (US Only)
prerequisites.notes: Prerequisite listing name differs from event header 'Classified Briefings'

tlos: TLO 1. The student will recall concepts of advanced weapons.

events.description: This 1-hour academic lecture will introduce students to the principles of advanced, network-enabled weapons test techniques.

tlos: TLO 1. Explain the goal of Electromagnetic Warfare and the characteristics of the EW environment.
TLO 2. Describe Electromagnetic Attack, Electromagnetic Protect, Electromagnetic Support.
TLO 3. Describe the enemy 'Kill Chain' (targeted against us).
TLO 4. Describe how different types of radars contribute information to an Integrated Air Defense System (IADS).
TLO 5. Abstract the impact of threat system modernization on the threat improvement/countermeasure improvement cycle.

events.description: This 7-hour academic lecture introduces the student to basics of Electromagnetic Warfare (EW) and EW test and evaluation. The course then builds on the RADAR and communications courses with respect to those electromagnetic properties that support information flow to various sensors and how EW techniques can be used to interfere with that information flow. The course introduces the concept of Integrated Air Defense Systems (IADS) and the 'kill chain' used by both offensive and defensive weapons. The course continues with Electromagnetic Warfare and EW testing, followed by Low Observable (L-O) technology and L-O testing.

events.eventName: Electronic Warfare Simulator for Cleared Students (F-22 SIL)
events.description: This simulator builds on the academics from the fundamentals and EW lecture to provide hands-on experience testing active electronic attack equipment in a laboratory environment. US students participate in a 2-hour simulator at the F-22 SIL to demonstrate concepts, capabilities, and test techniques in a multi-ship, elevated security setting.

events.eventName: Electronic Warfare Lab for Uncleared Students (Mission Systems Lab)

modules.moduleName: Kill Web Mission Systems Exams and Special Topics
tlos: TLO 1. The student will synthesize mission systems knowledge.

events.eventName: Kill Web Mission Systems End-of-Course Exam
Kill Web Mission Systems End-of-Course Debrief

events.description: This is a Phase of presentations on cutting-edge test techniques and technical details currently in execution throughout the Air Force Test Center.

modules.description: The 9000-level is an ungraded level to allow students to go deeper into specific classified program test campaigns.

tlos: TLO 1. The student will understand selected unique mission areas and techniques being used to test modern mission systems.
TLO 2. The student will be able to communicate test capabilities and how they meet the needs of warfighter/program offices: test ranges, organizations, and data processing.

events.description: These events provide an overview of US Only capabilities. This block provides students with the information they will need to participate in classified lectures and activities.
The students will participate in tours and receive hands-on experience in flight simulators with next generation fighter, bomber, and autonomous aircraft.
